text.title: Anti-Slop Rubric Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules every Anti-Slop Rubric application must enforce. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them. Plus a skip-methodology rule for decision-tree leaves that exit the methodology.

rule.statement: Apply the rubric to a single content artefact. Never batch multiple unrelated pieces through one pass.
rule.rationale: Batching turns a 1-decision artefact into a debate club; each instance gets its own record so the rationale is traceable.

rule.statement: Every input field carries a declared type AND a source URL/path. Free-form prose without a source is rejected.
rule.rationale: Untyped inputs let LLMs hallucinate plausible-but-wrong values; typed + sourced inputs make the output falsifiable.

rule.statement: Output names a single accountable owner (handle, email, or org role). No 'team' or 'we' as owner.
rule.rationale: Plural ownership equals no ownership; future readers need a named human to chase when the artefact decays.

rule.statement: Output carries semver-style version and last_reviewed date. Records older than 90 days without a review must be flagged on read.
rule.rationale: Methodology outputs decay; versioning + review-by-date forces refresh cadence.

rule.statement: Define the automated detector (lint rule, test name, metric query) BEFORE writing the corrective action.
rule.rationale: Writing the fix first creates rules with no detector; the detector enforces the rule going forward.

rule.statement: Attribution windows are declared up front and frozen for the duration of the test.
rule.rationale: Adjusting the attribution window after seeing data manufactures wins; pre-declared windows keep the readout falsifiable.

rule.statement: Skip this methodology when the decision tree resolves to a skip branch — preconditions are not met OR an adjacent methodology fits better.
rule.rationale: Skipping cleanly preserves the integrity of the rubric; misapplied methodologies produce false positives downstream.
